facts.fact_text: With K classes, we use a symmetric version of the multiclass logistic model (4.17) on page 119: Pr(G = k|X = x) = exp(βk0 + x^Tβk)/Σ exp(βl0 + x^Tβl)
Pr(G = k|X = x) = exp(βk0 + x^Tβk)/Σ exp(βl0 + x^Tβl)

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high variance and overfitting are a major concern in this setting. As a result, simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.
A version of multiclass logistic regression where all K classes have their own coefficient vectors, with regularization forcing Σˆβ_lj = 0.
In the "one versus one" (ovo) approach, we compute all pairwise classifiers. For each test point, the predicted class is the one that wins the most pairwise contests.
A method for generalizing two-class classifiers to K > 2 classes by computing all pairwise classifiers and using voting to determine the predicted class.

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high variance and overfitting are a major concern in this setting. As a result, simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.

In the "one versus all" (ova) approach, each class is compared to all of the others in K two-class comparisons. To classify a test point, we compute the confidences (signed distance from the hyperplane) for each of the K classifiers. The winner is the class with the highest confidence.
To classify a test point, we compute the confidences (signed distance from the hyperplane) for each of the K classifiers. The winner is the class with the highest confidence.

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high variance and overfitting are a major concern in this setting. As a result, simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.
A method for generalizing two-class classifiers to K > 2 classes by comparing each class to all others in K two-class comparisons, choosing the class with highest confidence.

The fused lasso (Tibshirani et al., 2005) solves min_β Σ(yi - Σβjxij)^2 + λ1Σ|βj| + λ2Σ|βj - βj-1|. The first penalty encourages the solution to be sparse, while the second encourages it to be smooth in the index j.
min_β Σ(yi - Σβjxij)^2 + λ1Σ|βj| + λ2Σ|βj - βj-1|
The first penalty encourages the solution to be sparse, while the second encourages it to be smooth in the index j.
A modification of the lasso for functional data that penalizes both the L1 norm of coefficients and the L1 norm of their successive differences, encouraging sparsity and smoothness.
simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.
Using this, we define the inner product K(x1,x2) = Σ_{a∈A^m} φ_a(x1)φ_a(x2) which measures the similarity between the two strings x1, x2.
K(x1,x2) = Σ_{a∈A^m} φ_a(x1)φ_a(x2)
A similarity measure for variable-length strings based on counting matching substrings, computing inner products K(x1,x2) = Σφ_a(x1)φ_a(x2) where φ_a counts occurrences of substring a.
We extracted all unique words from these abstracts, and defined features xij to be the number of times word j appears in abstract i. This is the so-called bag of words representation.
A document representation where features x_ij are counts of how many times word j appears in document i, discarding word order.

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in genomics and other areas of computational biology.
Thus we compute the eigenvectors of the double-centered kernel (I −M)K(I −M) for the principal components from an uncentered inner-product matrix.
(I - M)K(I - M)
Computing principal components from an uncentered inner-product matrix by centering: (I - M)K(I - M) where M is the mean operator.

Supervised principal components is a simple approach to regression and generalized regression that is especially useful when p ≫N. We restrict attention to features which by themselves have a sizable correlation with the outcome.
We restrict attention to features which by themselves have a sizable correlation with the outcome.
A method for high-dimensional regression that filters features by univariate significance, then computes principal components on the reduced set, and uses them in a regression model.

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in genomics and other areas of computational biology. We will see that high variance and overfitting are a major concern in this setting. As a result, simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.
Figure 18.12 shows the survival curve estimated by the Kaplan–Meier method for the 80 patients in the test set.
A method for estimating survival curves from censored survival data.

In this chapter we discuss prediction problems in which the number of features p is much larger than the number of observations N, often written p ≫N. Such problems have become of increasing importance, especially in genomics and other areas of computational biology.

concept_name: latent variable model for supervised pca

facts.fact_text: Suppose we have a response variable Y which is related to an underlying latent variable U by a linear model Y = β20 + β21U + ε. In addition, we have measurements on a set of features Xj indexed by j ∈P (for pathway), for which Xj = β10j + β11jU + ϵj.
Y = β20 + β21U + ε, Xj = β10j + β11jU + ϵj
A single-component factor analysis model where outcome Y relates to latent factor U, and relevant features X_j also relate to U through linear models.

We select the features as in steps (1) and (2a) of supervised principal components, but then apply PLS (rather than principal components) to these features. For our current discussion, we call this "thresholded PLS."
A variant of partial least squares where features are thresholded based on their correlation with the outcome before applying PLS, similar to supervised principal components.
simple, highly regularized approaches often become the methods of choice.

Then for interpretational purposes, as well as for practical uses, we would like some way of finding a reduced a set of features that approximates the model. Pre-conditioning (Section 18.6.3) is one way of doing this.
A method for finding a reduced set of features that approximates a model when the threshold yields too many features for interpretation.

In this approach, one first computes the supervised principal component predictor ŷi for each observation in the training set (with the threshold selected by cross-validation). Then we apply the lasso with ŷi as the outcome variable, in place of the usual outcome yi.
Then we apply the lasso with ŷi as the outcome variable, in place of the usual outcome yi.

A variant of the lasso where the outcome variable is first denoised using supervised principal components, then the lasso is applied with the denoised outcome as the target. All features are used in the lasso fit, not just those retained in thresholding.

An attractive alternative approach is to use the permutation distribution, since it avoids assumptions about the distribution of the data. We compute (in principle) all K = (58 choose 14) permutations of the sample labels, and for each permutation k compute the t-statistics tk_j.
since it avoids assumptions about the distribution of the data.
A nonparametric approach to computing p-values by permuting sample labels and computing test statistics for each permutation, avoiding distributional assumptions.
The family-wise error rate (FWER) is the probability of at least one false rejection, and is a commonly used overall measure of error.
FWER = Pr(V ≥ 1)
The probability of making at least one false rejection among all hypothesis tests, a conservative measure of overall error in multiple testing.
Benjamini and Hochberg (1995) first proposed the notion of false discovery rate, and gave a testing procedure (Algorithm 18.2) whose FDR is bounded by a user-defined level α.
a testing procedure (Algorithm 18.2) whose FDR is bounded by a user-defined level α.
A procedure for controlling false discovery rate at level α by ordering p-values and finding the largest index where p(j) ≤ jα/M, then rejecting all hypotheses with smaller p-values.

concept_name: plug-in estimate of false discovery rate

facts.fact_text: The total number of permutation values |tk_j| equal or greater than 4.101 is 1518, for an average of 1518/1000 = 1.518 per permutation. Thus a direct estimate of the false discovery rate is FDR̂ = 1.518/11 ≈ 14%.
FDR̂ = (# permutation values ≥ C) / (# observed values ≥ C)
A direct estimate of FDR computed as the average number of permutation values exceeding a threshold divided by the number of observed values exceeding that threshold.

The significance analysis of microarrays (SAM) approach offers a way of doing this. The basis of the SAM method is shown in Figure 18.20. On the vertical axis we have plotted the ordered test statistics t(1) ≤ t(2) ≤ ... ≤ t(M), while the horizontal axis shows the expected order statistics from the permutations of the data.
On the vertical axis we have plotted the ordered test statistics t(1) ≤ t(2) ≤ ... ≤ t(M), while the horizontal axis shows the expected order statistics from the permutations of the data.

A method for computing asymmetric significance cutpoints by plotting ordered test statistics against expected order statistics from permutations and finding where points leave a band of width Δ from the 45° line.
First we need to define the positive false discovery rate (pFDR) as
pFDR = E[V/R | R > 0]
A variant of FDR defined as E[V/R | R > 0], the expected false discovery rate conditional on making at least one rejection.
We define a random variable Zj which equals 0 if the jth null hypothesis is true, and 1 otherwise. We assume that each pair (tj, Zj) are i.i.d random variables with
Pr(t_j, Z_j) = π0 F0(t_j) + (1-π0) F1(t_j)
A Bayesian framework where each test statistic comes from either the null distribution F0 with probability π0 or the alternative distribution F1 with probability 1-π0.
We assume that each pair (tj, Zj) are i.i.d random variables.
